rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NORMA GALIANO 1987 TRUST
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is entered by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. (Part 1 of 3) Norma Galiano established the Norma L. Galiano Revocable Trust. Norma had two children: Leslie Roberts and Keith Galiano. Under the latest iteration of the Trust, Norma was the beneficiary during her life. Norma died a couple of years ago. Now, the beneficiaries are her children, though in unequal parts-Galiano receives substantially more than Roberts. Before the court is Roberts' First Amended Petition challenging the last two Trust amendments as the products of Galiano's undue influence. Galiano now seeks summary judgment, arguing the challenged Trust unequivocally reflects Norma Galiano's intent and, in any event, the challenged amendments do not confer an undue benefit upon him. Roberts contends that triable issues of material fact preclude summary judgment. Galiano's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. A motion for summary judgment shall be granted "if all the papers submitted show that there is no triable issue as to any material fact and that the moving party is entitled to a judgment as a matter of law." (Code Civ. Proc., 437c, subd. (c).) "In determining if the papers show that there is no triable issue as to any material fact, the court shall consider all of the evidence set forth in the papers, except the evidence to which objections have been made and sustained by the court, and all inferences reasonably deducible from the evidence, except summary judgment shall not be granted by the court based on inferences reasonably deducible from the evidence if contradicted by other inferences or evidence that raise a triable issue as to any material fact." (Ibid.) A judge's function at summary judgment is simply to decide if disputed issues of material fact make a trial necessary; the judge does not weigh the evidence or assess credibility. (Schmidt v Superior Court (2020) 44 Cal.App.5th 570, 583.) The moving party bears the "initial burden of production to make a prima facie showing of the nonexistence of any triable issue of material fact." (Falcon v. Long Beach Genetics, Inc. (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 1263, 1271). If the moving defendant carries their burden, "[t]he party opposing the summary judgment must make an independent showing by a proper declaration or by reference to a deposition or another discovery product that there is sufficient proof of the matters alleged to raise a triable question of fact if the moving party's evidence, standing alone, is sufficient to entitle the party to judgment. [Citations.] To avoid summary judgment, admissible evidence presented to the trial court, not merely claims or theories, must reveal a triable, material factual issue. [Citation.]" (Wiz Technology, Inc. v. Coopers & Lybrand (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 1, 10-11.) The initial burden shifting applies to the court's examination of evidence and expert witness testimony offered in support and opposition of the motion. Expert declarations in support of an MSJ are required to have a detailed, reasoned explanation for the expert opinion; an expert opinion submitted in opposition to an MSJ need not meet this requirement and must be liberally construed. "[D]eclarations need not prove the opposition's case; they suffice if they disclose the existence of a triable issue." (Jennifer C. v. Los Angeles Unified Sch. Dist. (2008) 168 Cal. App. 4th 1320, 1333, quoting other case law.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NORMA GALIANO 1987 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Judgment (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 3) Galiano moves for judgment on Roberts' undue influence claims. The elements of an undue influence claim under the common law are, generally: (1) the decedent's mental or physical condition was such as to be vulnerable to subversion of their freewill; (2) the provisions of the instrument are "unnatural" or "undue"; (3) the disposition is at variance with the decedent's intent, expressed before or after its execution; (4) a confidential relationship between the decedent and alleged influencer that afforded the latter an opportunity to influence the testamentary act; and (5) the alleged influencer's active participation in procuring the testamentary documents. (See, e.g., Lingenfelter's Estate (1952) 38 Cal.2d 571; Estate of Baker (1982) 131 Cal.App.3d 471, 480.) The determination of undue influence in procuring a testamentary instrument does not depend on any one element alone, but on the "probative force" of the elements in combination. (In re Lingenfelter's Estate, supra, 38 Cal.2d at p. 585; Estate of Baker, supra, 131 Cal.App.3d at p. 481.) Case law emphasizes that the essence of the undue influence inquiry is whether the alleged influencer "actively participated" in procurement of the testamentary document. (In re Lingenfelter's Estate, supra, 38 Cal.2d at p. 586; Estate of Baker, supra, 131 Cal.App.3d at p. 481.) "[A]ctivity on the part of the [alleged influencer] in procuring execution of the will may be established by inference, that is, by circumstantial evidence." (Ibid.) A finding of undue influence "need not be supported by direct evidence at the moment decedent signed the trust instruments" (Lintz v. Lintz (2014) 222 Cal.App.4th 1346, 1355), but can be proven "not only by indirect evidence but by proof of facts from which undue influence may be inferred" (Estate of Butcher (1941) 48 Cal.App.2d 465, 474). Here, triable issues of material fact preclude summary judgment. After making credibility determinations, resolving conflicts and drawing reasonable inferences, a reasonable trier of fact could find that the challenged amendments are the product of undue influence and should not be enforced. (See Petitioner's Response to Separate Statement of Undisputed Facts ("RSUF") 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 19, 20, 25, 26, 33; Petitioner's Statement of Additional Undisputed Facts ("PSAF") 34, 36, 37, 59, 63, 66, 67, 75). Galiano contends that Roberts cannot establish "that the [challenged Trust amendments] resulted from coercion or were inconsistent with voluntary action[.]" (Notice of Motion, 2:1-2.) As noted, the record contains evidence that could support a finding of undue influence. Galiano's argument that the record is devoid of evidence that he actively participated in the Trust amendments fails. If Roberts' evidence is credited and inferences are drawn in her favor, a reasonable trier of fact could conclude that Galiano was actively involved in procuring the fifth and sixth amendments, and the power and control he exercised amounted to undue influence over the testamentary act. The trier of fact, alternatively, may decide that Galiano did not actively participate. Either way, the fact trier's decisions will be informed by credibility determinations, weighing of evidence and inferences made. These are precisely the types of decisions this court cannot make on summary judgment.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NORMA GALIANO 1987 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Judgment (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 3) In his MPA, Galiano argues that Roberts cannot establish that the gifts made to him in the fifth and sixth amendments result in an "undue benefit." (Galiano's MPA at pp. 4-7.) Again, Galiano cites evidence, if credited, that would support a finding that the gifts to him were, in fact, not undue. But the record is more extensive, and the court must consider the record as a whole. The benefit inquiry, like the influence inquiry, entails "a qualitative assessment of the relationship between the decedent and the [influencer]" and "every case must be viewed in its own particular setting." (See Estate of Sarabia (1990) 221 Cal.App.3d 599, 607, superseded by statute on other grounds as stated in Rice v. Clark (2002) 28 Cal.4th 89.) The trier of fact may consider a broad array of evidence, including, for example, dispositional provisions in previous instruments, past expressions of the decedent's testamentary intentions, any showing of the extent to which the proponent would benefit in the absence of the challenged instrument. (See Sarabia, supra, at pp. 607-608; see also Estate of Gelonese (1974) 36 Cal.App.3d 854, 866-867 [evidence was sufficient to sustain the determination that a will would result in undue profit to the proponents where it showed it did not treat the decedent's children equally and there was evidence, though conflicting, that the decedent wanted them to share equally in her estate].) Here, the record evidence supports competing inferences and, therefore, the decision must be left to a trier of fact. In any event, Galiano's focus on elements in isolation is misplaced. The undue influence inquiry turns on the "probative force" of the elements in combination. (See In re Lingenfelter's Estate, supra, 38 Cal.2d at p. 585; Estate of Baker, supra, 131 Cal.App.3d at p. 481.) Here, considering the evidence as a whole, a reasonable trier of fact could find that the fifth and sixth amendments are the product of undue influence. To the extent they address evidence discussed above, Roberts' and Galiano's evidentiary objections are OVERRULED. To the extent the objections address evidence not discussed above, they are OVERRULED AS MOOT. Petitioner shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above, prior to the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on May 26, 2022.
